title: High-growth firms’ contribution to aggregate productivity growth
abstract: Abstract This paper investigates the contribution of high-growth firms (HGFs) to aggregate productivity growth, using Hungarian firm-level data. Three stylized facts emerge. First, output-based HGFs substantially outperform employment-based ones in terms of their productivity contribution: on average, sales-based HGFs contribute 5 times as much as employment-based ones. Further, the contribution of employment-based HGFs is negative in 48-50% of industry-years, compared to 25-31% for sales-based HGFs. Second, HGFs tend to contribute to productivity growth only during their high-growth phase but not afterwards. Third, HGFs’ contribution to productivity growth is higher in industries with more effective reallocation and with more young firms, but none of these are strong predictors of the HGFs’ contribution. Finally, we present a simple benchmark model to show that these patterns arise naturally under realistic correlation structures.
summary_en: High-growth firms (HGFs) are often held up as engines of productivity growth. But are they really? This paper uses Hungarian firm-level data to decompose the contribution. Three stylised facts: (1) sales-based HGFs contribute 5 times as much to aggregate productivity as employment-based HGFs; (2) HGFs contribute to productivity growth only during their high-growth phase, not afterward; (3) HGF contributions are larger in industries with effective reallocation and more young firms. An important corrective to HGF-centric innovation policy.
data_used: Hungarian firm-level administrative data. Definitions of HGFs by sales-growth and employment-growth with five-year and shorter windows.
policy_relevance: Directly relevant to Hungarian SME policy and innovation-strategy (ITM, NRDI Office). The paper's key message: policies should target sales-based HGFs in reallocation-friendly industries, not just employment-based growth. Relevant to the Hungarian State Holding Company (MNV) portfolio strategy and to SME subsidy targeting. Caveat: focuses on short-term productivity effects; the wider-economy benefits of fast-growing firms (knowledge spillovers, competitive pressure) may not be fully captured.
